notes: The 343,905-byte XML contains sixteen represented content items, 4,807 L and 195 LG elements. Empty FIGURE/P structures preserve positions of printed image placeholders rather than manuscript miniatures.

next_steps: Inspect Morris's papers or EETS production records and compare distinctive readings against extant copies.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy of the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend did Morris transcribe?

next_steps: Consult current Caxton and Wynkyn de Worde bibliographies while preserving Morris's wording as attributed history of scholarship.
openQuestions.question: How should Morris's description of the 1493 printing as the third edition be reconciled with current catalog numbering?

next_steps: Inspect or order all target surfaces in MS Liturg. 104 and reconcile historical and modern foliation.
openQuestions.question: Why do the XML milestones run through f. 91r while DIMEV gives ff. 49–88 for Horae de Cruce?

next_steps: Collate printed pp. 222–224 against the manuscript surfaces and inspect XML line-container boundaries.
openQuestions.question: Does Morris's statement of seventeen couplets reflect a missing or merged line where the XML has 33 L elements?

next_steps: Inspect Royal MS 18 A X ff. 130v–134v and compare headings, catchwords, and Morris's lineation.
openQuestions.question: Why does the British Library description refer to an incomplete ending at line 333 while the XML has 335 L elements?

next_steps: Retain both with attribution and use the current catalog date for normalized witness metadata.
openQuestions.question: Should Morris's late-thirteenth-century dating of Ashmole 43 be retained alongside the current Bodleian first-quarter-fourteenth-century date?

next_steps: Ask British Library digitisation services whether existing images can be re-delivered before commissioning new photography.
openQuestions.question: Can current public image delivery be restored for Royal MS 17 A XXVII or Add MS 22029, both historically reported as digitized?

relations.subjectLabel: Digital Bodleian selected images for MS Liturg. 104

reviewNotes: This is an edition-defined selection, not one homogeneous medieval work or Morris's complete volume.
Numbered items II and XIII each contain two distinct witness versions; the selection therefore has thirteen main items or witness-versions plus three appendix items.

Raw XML representation metrics are 4,807 L and 195 LG. Item XIII's Royal stream includes twenty L elements inside two apparatus notes; the metric is not a claim that all 4,807 lines belong to uninterrupted medieval base text.

The Bodleian one-image objects for Fairfax 14, Douce 126, and Douce 128 are complete downloads of provider-published selections but do not contain the target loci. The Liturg. 104 selection contains one overlapping target surface.
Negative current-image findings for British Library witnesses and U-M delivery are date-bounded to 2026-07-16.

lineage.summary: The checked-in aha2702 XML copies University of Michigan CME item AHA2702.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR encoding of selected printed pages from Richard Morris's 1871 EETS Original Series 46 edition. The encoded selection contains thirteen main items or witness-versions and three appendix items: it retains Morris pp. 17–97 and 107–224, while excluding front matter, Old English item I, item V, and back matter. Morris directly edited distinct passages from seven Bodleian witnesses, six British Library witnesses, and the 20 May 1493 Wynkyn de Worde Golden Legend, while Harley MS 2277 and Additional MS 11748 served as collating witnesses; Additional 11748 also supplied a narrowly identified Royal-version passage and quoted apparatus. Complete public facsimiles of Ashmole 43 and the Vernon Manuscript, a complete scan of the 1871 EETS carrier, and all currently published Bodleian selected-image objects relevant to the other four Oxford witnesses are cached. Source, collation, physical-witness, facsimile, access, and rights layers remain separate.

catalogSummary.title: Legends of the Holy Rood: Symbols of the Passion and Cross-Poems

catalogingSubject.label: Legends of the Holy Rood: Symbols of the Passion and Cross-Poems
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is the edition-defined selection represented by University of Michigan item AHA2702.0001.001: thirteen medieval items or witness-versions from Richard Morris's numbered texts II–IV and VI–XIII, plus three appendix works, as encoded from printed pp. 17–97 and 107–224 of Morris's 1871 EETS volume. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, all versions of any constituent work, Morris's complete volume, the omitted Old English items I and V, the omitted front or back matter, the whole manuscripts or 1493 book from which the texts were edited, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.

contentParts.label: Main-text selection: Morris II–IV and VI–XIII

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[4], excluding DIV2[@TYPE='omitted Old English legend']; printed pp. 17–97 and 107–193

contentParts.summary: Container for thirteen represented items or witness-versions, excluding Old English items I and V; child work parts carry the additive item and line metrics.

contentParts.label: II. Þe Holy Rode — Ashmole version
contentParts.summary: A South English Legendary Holy Rood version edited from Ashmole 43 and collated with the imperfect Harley 2277 text.

contentParts.label: II. Hou þe Holy Cros Was Y-founde — Vernon version
contentParts.summary: The Vernon Manuscript version of the same broad Holy Rood tradition, printed in parallel with the Ashmole version.

contentParts.label: III. The Story of the Holy Rood

locators.value: ff. 76va–81r, within the Expanded Northern Passion at ff. 67r–86r

contentParts.summary: A Cross-tree narrative embedded in the Expanded Northern Passion in Harley 4196.

contentParts.label: IV. Finding of the Cross
contentParts.summary: A verse account for the feast of the Invention of the Cross from Harley 4196.

contentParts.label: VI. How þe Hali Cros Was Fundin be Seint Elaine
contentParts.summary: A northern verse Finding of the Cross text copied in Fairfax 14.

contentParts.label: VII. Exposition of the Holy Rood
contentParts.summary: A verse narrative for the feast of the Exaltation of the Cross from Harley 4196.

contentParts.label: VIII. Dispute Between Mary and the Cross — Vernon version
contentParts.summary: A stanzaic debate in which Mary and the Cross contest responsibility for Christ's suffering.

contentParts.label: IX. With an O and an I

locators.value: ff. 90v–91r according to Morris's milestone mapping; DIMEV gives f. 90va

contentParts.summary: A six-stanza Passion lyric with a recurring O-and-I refrain.

contentParts.label: X. With an O for an I
contentParts.summary: A short Passion lyric whose refrain pairs O and I.

contentParts.label: XI. The Invention of the Holy Cross
contentParts.summary: Middle English prose from the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend printing, edited by Morris as his item XI.

contentParts.label: XII. The Exaltation of the Cross
contentParts.summary: Middle English prose from the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend printing, edited by Morris as his item XII.

contentParts.label: XIII. The Symbols of the Passion — Royal version

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[4]/DIV2[13]/DIV3; 252 L, 29 LG, 26 P; printed pp. 170–196
ff. 72v–81r; O Vernicle ff. 72v–78v and thanksgiving prayer ff. 79r–80r, followed by further captions/text through f. 81r

contentParts.summary: A text-image Arma Christi sequence from Royal 17 A XXVII; the encoding preserves verse and captions but reduces the twenty-four miniature sequence to structural placeholders.

contentParts.label: XIII. The Symbols of the Passion — Additional 22029 version

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[4]/DIV2[14]/DIV3; 152 L, 24 LG, 24 empty P illustration placeholders; printed pp. 171–193

contentParts.summary: The Arma Christi roll version, whose twenty-four illustrations were supplied to Morris by Professor de la Motte; the encoding retains empty illustration placeholders rather than the pictures.

contentParts.label: Appendix
contentParts.summary: Container for three additional Middle English works from Royal 18 A X and Bodleian MS Liturg. 104; child work parts carry the additive item and line metrics.

contentParts.label: Dispute Between Mary and the Cross — Royal version
contentParts.summary: A longer Royal 18 A X version of the Mary-and-the-Cross debate, arranged against the corresponding stanzas of Morris's item VIII.

contentParts.label: Festivals of the Church
notes: The XML has 335 L elements. The British Library catalog describes the witness as incomplete at line 333; the difference likely reflects counting conventions and remains open.
contentParts.summary: A verse sequence on church feasts, incomplete at the end in Royal 18 A X.

contentParts.label: Horae de Cruce
notes: DIMEV gives a broader locus of ff. 49–88, while the encoding's seven milestone sequences extend through f. 91r; manuscript autopsy is needed to reconcile the conventions.
Morris describes seventeen couplets, while the XML has 33 L elements rather than 34; the discrepancy is not silently corrected.
contentParts.summary: English verse portions of the Hours of the Cross with the beginnings of preceding Latin prayers and rubrics, selected discontinuously from Bodleian MS Liturg. 104.

dateStatements.display: Represented textual states are attested from about 1300 or the early fourteenth century through the 1493 Golden Legend printing
notes: This range dates witnesses and the source print, not composition of all constituent works. Morris dated Ashmole 43 to the late thirteenth century, while the current Bodleian catalog dates it to the first quarter of the fourteenth.

scope.description: First publication of Morris's edition-defined selection
Date of the exact printed edition used by Morris

extent.description: The cataloged hierarchy has two editorial containers and sixteen represented intellectual items or witness-versions: thirteen in the main text and three in the appendix. The main text contributes 4,067 L elements in 129 LG containers and the appendix 740 L elements in 66 LG containers, for 4,807 L and 195 LG overall. Form classification counts thirteen verse-only items, two prose-only Golden Legend items, and one mixed Horae item. Sixty-eight represented P elements comprise ten Golden Legend prose paragraphs, fifty Symbols illustration/text placeholders, and eight Horae paragraphs or rubric-like blocks; P is not an item count. Five further empty P placeholders belong to omitted item V, and three P elements outside the represented divisions belong to carrier/header/title structures, accounting for the XML-wide total of 76 P. The XML also has 272 PB and 151 MILESTONE elements, but these are carrier and source-locus scaffolding rather than additional intellectual items. Printed-page coverage is discontinuous: pp. 17–97 and 107–224.

formStatements.description: The selection combines thirteen verse-only items, two prose-only Golden Legend items, and one mixed verse/prose Horae item.
Verse prayers with prose or rubric-like material and Latin incipits.

scope.description: The represented medieval texts, excluding embedded Latin and modern editorial matter
Arma Christi captions and incipits of prayers or offices

notes: Roman numerals follow Morris. Numbers I and V are absent because those Old English texts are outside the encoded selection, not because the cataloging omitted two represented works.
The two item-II versions, two item-XIII versions, and two Mary-and-the-Cross versions are counted separately because the edition and encoding transmit distinct textual states.
Illustration placeholders in the Symbols items are structural representation facts; they are not prose paragraphs and do not make those items mixed-form.
Manuscript dates are first-known-attestation evidence and are not silently converted into composition dates.

next_steps: Inspect Morris's papers or EETS production records and compare distinctive readings against extant copies.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy of the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend did Morris transcribe?

next_steps: Consult current Caxton and Wynkyn de Worde bibliographies; preserve both attributed descriptions meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: How should Morris's description of the 1493 printing as the third edition be reconciled with current catalogs that treat it as the second English edition or a reprint of 1483?

next_steps: Inspect all target leaves in MS Liturg. 104 or order images and reconcile modern and historical foliation.
openQuestions.question: Why do the XML milestones run through f. 91r while DIMEV gives ff. 49–88 for Horae de Cruce?

next_steps: Collate printed pp. 222–224 against the manuscript surfaces and inspect line-container boundaries.
openQuestions.question: Does Morris's statement of seventeen couplets reflect a missing or merged line where the XML has 33 L elements?

next_steps: Inspect Royal 18 A X ff. 130v–134v and compare catchwords, headings, and Morris's lineation.
openQuestions.question: Why does the British Library description refer to an incomplete ending at line 333 while the XML has 335 L elements?

next_steps: Retain both with attribution and use the current catalog date for present-day witness metadata.
openQuestions.question: Should the history-of-scholarship note retain Morris's late-thirteenth-century date alongside the current Bodleian first-quarter-fourteenth-century date?

summaries.value: An edition-defined anthology of Middle English traditions concerning the Holy Cross and Christ's Passion. It juxtaposes alternate manuscript versions of True Cross legends, poems on finding and exalting the Cross, two versions of a debate between Mary and the Cross, short Passion refrains, prose excerpts from the 1493 English Golden Legend, two text-image versions of the Symbols of the Passion, and three appendix works on Mary and the Cross, church festivals, and the Hours of the Cross.

The encoded selection preserves thirteen main-text items or witness-versions—Morris II twice, III, IV, VI, VII, VIII, IX, X, XI, XII, and XIII twice—and three appendix works. Front matter, Old English item I, Old English item V, and back matter are represented only by omitted-content scaffolding and are outside the cataloged subject.

Version labels are witness- and edition-specific. The two item-II texts, the two item-XIII texts, and the two Mary-and-the-Cross texts are not collapsed into single abstract works; Morris's direct sources, collation sources, digital surrogates, and the encoded selection remain separate in the lineage manifest.

It juxtaposes alternate manuscript versions of True Cross legends, poems on finding and exalting the Cross, two versions of a debate between Mary and the Cross, short Passion refrains, prose excerpts from the 1493 English Golden Legend, two text-image versions of the Symbols of the Passion, and three appendix works on Mary and the Cross, church festivals, and the Hours of the Cross.
